context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out the Spider Diagram assignment for my city case study. I’m not totally sure how to organize the city’s climate actions into the 5 pathways, so I could use help getting started.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—start simple. Don’t try to fill the whole diagram at once.

First, tell me: what is your case study city, and what are 2–3 climate actions, plans, or policies you’ve already found for it?

Then, from your course materials, what are the exact names of the 5 pathways? Once you list them, we can sort each action into the most fitting pathway one by one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and use guided discovery rather than explaining the whole assignment outright. To keep the student doing the thinking, I will ask a small number of targeted questions that help them identify their city, the 5 pathways framework they were given in the course, and the actions they have already found. This moves directly toward completing the Spider Diagram without writing it for them. I will keep the response brief and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student:
My city is New York City. I’ve found PlaNYC, the NYC Climate Resiliency Design Guidelines, and Local Law 97, but I’m a little fuzzy on how specific each one needs to be for the diagram.

I think the 5 pathways are something like infrastructure, governance, behavior, planning, and finance? I’m not confident I have the exact names right.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re asking the right question: the level of specificity matters.

Before sorting, verify the exact 5 pathway names from the course page—don’t rely on memory here. Can you check and paste them?

Then ask yourself for each NYC example: is it a broad citywide strategy, a decision tool/guideline, or a binding regulation? That distinction will help you decide how to place it. Which of your three feels most concrete to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will correct the student's uncertainty indirectly by prompting them to verify the exact pathway names in the course materials, since accuracy matters for the assignment. I will also help them think about level of specificity without assigning categories for them or supplying a completed mapping. The questions will narrow the task: verify framework, then decide whether each item is a broad plan, rule, or tool.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I was guessing, so I should check the exact names first. Of the three, Local Law 97 feels most concrete because it’s an actual binding regulation with building emissions limits, not just a general strategy.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a stronger way to think about it.

Now push one step further: what kind of mechanism is Local Law 97 using to drive climate action—rules, money, physical systems, planning, or changes in people/organizational practice?

Pick the best fit and explain your reasoning in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's useful distinction and push them to classify one example through its function, not by giving the category. This keeps the student reasoning toward the diagram. I will also keep the response short and centered on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’d put it under rules/governance, I think, because it sets legal emissions caps for large buildings and requires compliance. I’m hesitating a bit because it also changes building operations, so I’m not sure if I’m oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor:
That hesitation is normal, but don’t get stuck there. A policy can touch multiple pathways; your job is to choose the primary one.

Ask: what is LL97 first and foremost—a law, or an operations program?

Give me a primary pathway and a secondary pathway for it. Then we’ll do PlaNYC next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's reasoning while preventing overthinking by emphasizing best-fit categorization, since spider diagrams often simplify multifaceted policies. I will not supply the official category or final answer. Instead, I will ask the student to articulate a primary versus secondary effect and then move to the next item, keeping the exchange bite-sized and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: Okay, first and foremost it’s a law. So I’d say primary pathway = governance/regulation, and secondary pathway = changes in organizational practice or operations.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid distinction. Keep that “primary vs. secondary” logic.

Now PlaNYC: is it mainly a binding rule, a long-range strategic plan, a funding tool, a physical infrastructure intervention, or a behavior-change effort?

Choose one primary function and justify it in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student’s productive distinction and move them to the next item. I’ll use guided discovery by asking them to identify PlaNYC by its function and scope, without naming the pathway for them or providing wording for the diagram.

exchanges.student: I’d call PlaNYC a long-range strategic plan, since it lays out citywide climate and sustainability goals and directions rather than directly imposing legal requirements by itself.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That reasoning is clear and usable.

Now test the Guidelines the same way: are the Climate Resiliency Design Guidelines mainly telling the city how to make decisions, how to build physical projects, how to fund action, or how to change public behavior?

What is their main function?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student’s progress and continue the same classification pattern with the third item. This keeps the structure consistent and helps the student build a reusable method for the assignment. I will remain concise and avoid naming the official pathway labels for them.

exchanges.student: I think their main function is telling the city how to make decisions, since they guide agencies on how to account for climate risks in planning and design. I’m a tiny bit unsure because they affect projects too, but the guideline part seems more central.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a sensible reading. Don’t overvalue the downstream effects; focus on the main mechanism.

Now do the important check: pull up the course’s exact 5 pathway names and match each of your three NYC items to one official label.

Paste your 3 matches here. If one feels ambiguous, note the runner-up too.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's direction without finalizing categories for them, then prompt them to verify the official pathway names and align their three examples to those exact labels. This keeps the student responsible for the mapping and moves directly toward a usable draft. I will keep it concise and discourage overthinking secondary effects.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit stuck because I still don’t have the exact official labels in front of me. If I paste the 5 pathway names once I find them, can I try matching the three items and have you check if my labels make sense?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the right next step.

Find the exact 5 pathway names, paste them, and then make your own best-fit match for:
- PlaNYC
- Climate Resiliency Design Guidelines
- Local Law 97

I’ll check whether your reasoning is strong and point out any weak spots, but you need to make the first pass.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will encourage the student to verify the framework before proceeding, since using exact course terminology is necessary for the assignment. I will invite them to attempt the mapping themselves and offer evaluative feedback without taking over or supplying the answers. This keeps the interaction Socratic, brief, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll do that first pass once I pull the exact names. I think that’ll help a lot, because right now I’m mixing up my own categories with the course ones.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly. Right now the main problem is framework accuracy, not your reasoning.

So do this in order:
1. Get the exact 5 pathway names from the course.
2. Match each NYC item to one primary pathway.
3. Add a short reason for each.

When you have that first pass, send it. That’s the point where feedback will actually be useful.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's plan and keep the focus narrow: verify the official framework, then make a first-pass mapping. I will not add new content that could replace the student's work. The response stays brief, encouraging, and directed toward the next concrete action.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’ll grab the exact pathway names and come back with a first-pass match plus one short reason for each item.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the right move.

When you come back, send:
- the 5 exact pathway names
- your match for each of the 3 NYC items
- one short reason for each

Keep it rough. A first attempt is better than more guessing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should acknowledge the student's concrete next step and keep momentum without adding unnecessary scaffolding. The student is on track, so the best tutoring move is to reinforce the process and set a clear expectation for what to bring back. This stays Socratic by withholding the mapping and requiring the student to generate it first.
